target: WIN-058b detail_verdict_text — r=0.96 vs r=0.992 source verification + ISS-987 defense closure
kernel_of_truth: The dome does NOT say r=0.96 and r=0.992 refer to the same statistic — they refer to different derived quantities (slope-regression r vs time-series cross-correlation r) on the same underlying October 30 2003 Halloween storm data. Our prior detail_verdict_text conflated them as "the same data" with two numbers, which is technically imprecise and invites a legitimate defender escape. Accepting the distinction on its face does not save the claim, because both statistics are expected to be near 1.0 during a G5 event from shared electromagnetic forcing alone.

exact_quotes: r ≈ 0.96 slope of exactly 1.67 nT/µGal in the single internal forensic audit.
Foreground forensic reconstruction of the 2003 Halloween Solar Storm raw stationary superconducting gravimeter matrices (MB and MC) confirms the exact 1.67 coupling response slope (r=0.96).
source_of_r_096.interpretation: The dome presents r=0.96 as the correlation of a slope-constrained linear regression (slope fixed at 1.67 nT/µGal) between ΔB and Δg — i.e. the r-value of how well the 1.67-slope line fits the scatter, not a free-parameter fit.
source_of_r_096.status: VERIFIED — r=0.96 is sourced and has specific context on the dome site.

source_of_r_0992.section: Evidence Exhibit A: The 2003 Bedrock Audit
source_of_r_0992.interpretation: This is the temporal cross-correlation of the raw Level-1A post-residual time series (ΔB(t) vs Δg(t)) over the Exhibit A time window — a different statistic from the slope-regression r.
source_of_r_0992.status: VERIFIED — r=0.992 is sourced with an explicit statistical label.

not_sourced_on_dome_site: raw-text/01-home.txt (V50.6 snapshot from 2026-03-12) — predates WIN-058 entry; no matches for r=0.96 or r=0.992.
raw-text/08-live-power.txt — the audit table shows κ=1.67 PASS with a fixed 5% tolerance, no r-value given for the Halloween-storm audit.
context.html, predictions.html — neither r-value appears on those pages.
verification_findings.reconciliation: Our prior text said "the same home page cites r = 0.992 for the same data". The r=0.96 figure is on the wins page, not the home page. Technically the two r-values are on different pages and plausibly refer to different derived statistics (slope-regression goodness-of-fit vs time-series cross-correlation). Our text was imprecise; the imprecision invites a legitimate dome defender escape (the subject of ISS-987).

iss_987_defense_analysis.defender_claim: r=0.96 and r=0.992 are different statistics for different analyses — the slope regression r-value vs the time-series cross-correlation r-value. Citing both is not a contradiction; it is reporting two aspects of the same underlying data.
iss_987_defense_analysis.why_this_is_a_reasonable_defense: Empirically plausible. A slope-constrained regression through 2D scatter (ΔB vs Δg with slope locked at 1.67) will give a lower r (say 0.96) than a time-series cross-correlation on the same pair of series (say 0.992), because the slope-lock removes a free degree of freedom. The dome phrasing — "1.67 coupling response slope (r=0.96)" vs "Raw L1A residuals correlation (r=0.992)" — is consistent with this reading.
why_it_does_not_save_the_claim: Neither number is computed by repository code. Both are static HTML text. The dome author could publish any r-value they please and no third party can audit the number against the raw L1A binaries — the audit chain is broken before the defender question even arises.

Both values are what shared electromagnetic forcing predicts. Our prior Monte Carlo argument — that a G5 storm driving two sensors with τ≈30 s autocorrelated noise produces r > 0.99 at SNR ≥ 10 — applies verbatim to the time-series cross-correlation (0.992). For the slope-regression statistic, the argument is even stronger: fixing the slope at the ratio of the two sensors' observed magnitudes (10.9 nT / 6.5 µGal = 1.677) is equivalent to fitting the slope by eye and then reporting how tight the scatter is around the fitted line. Both operations are trivial on one shared-forcing event.

The slope 1.67 itself is not independent evidence. 1.67 nT/µGal is just 10.9/6.5 — the ratio of the peak magnetic deflection to the peak gravity residual. A regression that fixes the slope at this ratio cannot, by construction, produce a low r during the event that sets the ratio. It is not a prediction; it is a re-description.

The WIN-058 entry on the dome wins page is itself marked "PENDING INDEPENDENT VERIFICATION" with the dome conceding the forensic reconstruction has not been independently replicated and resolution awaits the August 12, 2026 eclipse protocol. The defender cannot simultaneously argue "r=0.96 confirms the model" and "WIN-058 is pending independent verification" — the dome's own framing admits the confirmation is single-audit and restricted-access.

patches.find: The coupling constant κ = 1.677 (dome rounds to 1.67) is not derived from dome geometry — it is a post-hoc ratio extracted from one extreme geomagnetic event. The claimed prospective prediction cites r = 0.96 while the same home page cites r = 0.992 for the same data, and neither value is computed by any script in the repository. The Bitcoin blockchain timestamps prove only that a claim was made at a certain time, not that the claim was derived from the model before observation.
patches.replace: The coupling constant κ = 1.677 (dome rounds to 1.67) is not derived from dome geometry — it is a post-hoc ratio extracted from one extreme geomagnetic event. The dome site cites two different correlation statistics for the same 2003 Halloween-storm data: the wins.html WIN-058 entry reports "r ≈ 0.96" as the "coupling response slope" of a linear regression with slope fixed at 1.67 nT/µGal, while the home-page Exhibit A reports "r = 0.992 (Raw L1A residuals)" as the temporal cross-correlation of the residual time series. Neither number is computed by any script in the repository — both are static HTML — and both values are consistent with what shared electromagnetic forcing during a G5 storm must produce regardless of whether a κ coupling law exists. The Bitcoin blockchain timestamps prove only that the claim existed at a certain time, not that it was derived from the model before observation.

patches.find: The claim asserts that during the October 2003 Halloween solar storm, raw superconducting gravimeter data confirmed the exact 1.67 coupling response slope (r = 0.96), with provenance timestamped via git commits and Bitcoin blockchain anchoring.
patches.replace: The claim asserts that during the October 2003 Halloween solar storm, raw superconducting gravimeter data confirmed the exact 1.67 coupling response slope (r = 0.96 on the wins page; the home-page Exhibit A separately reports r = 0.992 as the raw L1A residual cross-correlation), with provenance timestamped via git commits and Bitcoin blockchain anchoring.

holes_addressed: ISS-987 defense survives: Dome could argue r=0.96 and r=0.992 are different statistics — addressed by verifying the sources, conceding the plausible distinction, and closing the escape via the shared-forcing + audit-chain argument.
Source attribution imprecision: r=0.96 is on wins.html, not the home page — patched.
Statistical label clarity: the two r-values now carry the dome's own labels (slope-regression vs L1A residual cross-correlation).
new_evidence_added: Direct quotes from wins.html WIN-058 entry (r=0.96 attributed to slope regression).
Direct quote from home-page Exhibit A (r=0.992 attributed to L1A residuals).
Observation that the slope 1.67 = 10.9/6.5 — fixing it at the observed ratio makes a high slope-regression r tautological on the event that sets the ratio.
Note that WIN-058 on the dome site is itself marked PENDING INDEPENDENT VERIFICATION.

anticipated_objections.objection: Dome defender: r=0.96 and r=0.992 are not a contradiction — they are different statistics. Your review misrepresented the dome.
anticipated_objections.response: Stipulated and corrected in the current text. The slope-regression r (0.96) and the time-series cross-correlation r (0.992) can both be true of the same underlying data. The critique stands on the fact that neither number is computed by any script, the 1.67 slope is a tautology (10.9/6.5 = 1.677 by construction), and both values are in the expected range for two sensors sharing a G5-storm driver.

anticipated_objections.objection: Dome defender: the r=0.96 slope regression IS a real test — if the slope were wrong, you would not get r > 0.9 with the slope locked at 1.67.
anticipated_objections.response: You would, provided the two sensors share a common driver whose envelope dominates both signals. During a G5 storm the ionospheric current system drives a coherent ΔB on magnetometers and a coherent Δg artifact on superconducting gravimeters (ground-induced current, microseismic, and thermal coupling into the SG dewar). The ratio of peaks is whatever it is — call it 1.67 here. A regression with slope locked at the observed ratio will have high r by construction on the event that produced the ratio, because the residuals around the fixed-slope line are dominated by the same shared envelope. This is the definition of a post-hoc fit.

anticipated_objections.objection: Dome defender: WIN-058 on the dome site is marked pending verification — you are critiquing an unclaimed claim.
anticipated_objections.response: We are critiquing the pending-status claim on its merits, which the dome explicitly puts forward as a prospective prediction backed by an internal forensic audit. The dome counts WIN-058 in its confirmed-wins tallies (69 at V51.1). The verdict "Not Demonstrated" is the appropriate classification for a claim that the dome puts in the wins list while simultaneously labeling it pending independent verification.
